content.text: Structured output from a language model can be obtained in two fundamentally different ways: either by constraining the model's token-level sampling to match a JSON grammar, or by framing the output as an argument object for a declared tool.

— is the dominant pattern in production agentic systems because it combines schema enforcement with action semantics: the model does not merely emit data, it expresses intent to invoke a specific capability.
Function calling is a protocol supported by major LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, Mistral, and others) that allows a caller to declare a set of

— each described by a JSON Schema for its input parameters — and instruct the model to respond with a structured invocation of one or more of those tools instead of, or in addition to, free-form text.

(Anthropic) block containing: the name of the tool chosen, and a JSON object of arguments that conforms to the declared schema. The calling code extracts these arguments, validates them, executes the real function, and feeds the result back into the conversation. The model then uses the result to continue reasoning or produce a final answer.

Crucially, the model never actually executes anything — it only produces a structured declaration of what it would like to call. All side effects remain under the orchestrator's control, which is why function calling is equally useful for pure data extraction (no real tool, just parse the arguments) and for genuine action execution (send email, query database, call API).

Early LLM integrations relied on carefully crafted prompts that instructed the model to "respond in JSON" or "always use this format." This created a fragile dependency between prompt wording and output shape. Any ambiguity in the prompt — or any hallucination by the model — could produce unparseable output or silently incorrect fields, requiring defensive parsing logic and retry loops throughout application code.
Function calling eliminates most of that brittleness by moving the contract from natural-language instructions into machine-readable JSON Schema. The model is trained to respect
fields, which dramatically reduces the failure rate for structured extraction tasks compared to prompt-only approaches.
At the architectural level, function calling is the mechanism that transforms a text-completion model into an

: once the model can declare intent (call this tool with these arguments), an orchestrator can execute arbitrary actions, chain results, and continue multi-turn reasoning loops. Without function calling, agents would need to parse model-emitted text or Markdown to discover actions — a parsing problem that never fully goes away without a formal schema protocol.

Parameter types, enums, and required fields are part of the tool definition. The model produces arguments that match the schema far more reliably than free-text prompting alone, and any residual violations can be caught at the application layer before execution.
to execute. Security policies, rate limits, retries, and capability checks belong in the orchestrator, not in the prompt.
Modern APIs (OpenAI parallel tool calls, Anthropic Claude) allow the model to emit multiple
in one response. The orchestrator can execute them concurrently, reducing round-trip latency in workflows that need several independent lookups.
Pure data extraction without a real tool.
Declaring a tool purely to receive structured arguments is a widely used pattern: define a
to force its use, and the model's argument object is your structured output — no prompt gymnastics required.

The conversation history includes all previous tool calls and their results, giving the model full context to plan subsequent actions, detect errors in prior steps, and adapt its strategy without external orchestration logic.

Because function calling is part of the API contract rather than a prompt convention, providers can optimize for it (fine-tuning, latency, accuracy) and add features (streaming tool calls, grounding) that would be impossible to support via freeform JSON modes.

Tool definitions are injected into the system context on every request. For wide toolsets (20+ tools with verbose descriptions and schemas), this can add thousands of tokens per call, increasing both latency and cost.

Each tool call requires at minimum one additional API call (tool result appended, model called again). Complex tasks that need 5–10 tool calls can have 10–20 API round-trips before delivering a final answer.

A poorly designed tool schema — ambiguous descriptions, missing enum constraints, overlapping responsibilities between tools — will lead the model to pick the wrong tool, supply unexpected argument values, or fail to call tools when it should. Schema quality directly determines reliability.
(the default), the model can decide to respond with text instead of calling a tool. For workflows that absolutely require a structured output, callers must set
to force a specific tool or require any tool call.

OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google each use slightly different field names and response shapes for tool calls. Libraries like LangChain or LiteLLM normalize these, but direct API usage requires per-provider adaptation code.

Schema validation checks structure and type, not semantic correctness. A model might supply a syntactically valid but logically wrong argument value — for example, a date in the correct ISO format but one year in the past.
At the protocol level, a function-calling request differs from a plain chat completion only in two additions: a
declares one callable with a name, a natural-language description, and a JSON Schema for its
When the model receives the request, it considers the conversation context and decides whether calling a tool would help. If so, it produces a response with
(Anthropic). The content of the response includes one or more tool invocation blocks, each with a unique call ID, the tool name, and a serialized JSON string of the chosen arguments.

The orchestrator then: (1) parses each invocation, (2) optionally validates the arguments against the schema it has on hand, (3) routes to the matching Python/JS function, (4) collects results, (5) appends each result to the messages list as a

-role message (keyed by the call ID), and (6) calls the model again. The model sees all prior turns, tool invocations, and results, and continues reasoning — possibly calling more tools or delivering a final assistant text.

The key insight is that the model never leaves the conversation loop. The entire multi-step plan is encoded in the message history, and the model's behavior at each step is fully conditioned on what tools returned, allowing error handling, retry decisions, and strategy adaptation all to happen within a single conversation context.
— the default; the model decides whether to call a tool or respond with text.
(Anthropic) — the model must call at least one tool.
— forces a specific tool, useful for pure data extraction where the tool name is just a schema container.

The diagram below shows the complete lifecycle of a function call, from the initial client request through schema validation, tool execution, and the second LLM pass that synthesizes the tool result into a final response.

attrs.alt: Function calling request/response lifecycle showing Client, LLM, tool_calls, Orchestrator, Tool Function, and the return path through a second LLM pass

content.text: Two LLM calls are shown because each tool call introduces exactly one model boundary: the first call produces the tool invocation, and the second call (after the result is appended) produces the assistant's final message. In practice, a single user turn may trigger many such cycles before the model chooses to stop calling tools.

The orchestrator is the most security-critical component in this diagram. It sits between the model's declared intent and the real execution environment, enforcing policies such as allowlisted tool names, per-user rate limits, argument sanitization, and execution timeouts.
You need structured data that maps naturally to a typed schema — entity extraction, classification with a known label set, form filling, or database record construction.

Your application needs to take actions: search a knowledge base, query an API, run code, send a notification. The function-calling protocol integrates action declaration and structured argument passing in one mechanism.
You are building a multi-step agent loop where the model must reason across multiple tool results before producing a final answer.
You want to allow the model to choose which of several capabilities to invoke based on context — the model selects from the declared tool set rather than always running a fixed pipeline.
You need parallel data fetching: multiple independent lookups (e.g., retrieve user profile AND order history simultaneously) before synthesizing a response.
The output schema is simple, always required, and static — JSON mode or response format with a schema may be lower latency and simpler to implement, with no concept of tools.
Toolset size exceeds 30–40 tools. Model accuracy at tool selection degrades with very large tool libraries. Prefer dynamic tool loading or tool routing at the orchestrator layer.
You need deterministic formatting of model-generated prose with embedded typed fields — structured output response formats are a better fit than fabricating a tool call.
Your provider does not support function calling (some local or open-source models lack reliable tool-use training).

The implementation has three responsibilities: defining tools, dispatching calls, and managing conversation state. The following steps describe a general pattern that applies across providers, with concrete code in the Examples section.
Define each tool as a JSON object with
JSON Schema. Use descriptive field names and include a
for every parameter — the model uses these descriptions to decide which argument values to supply.
Attach the tool list to each API request. For extraction-only flows, set
to force the target tool. For agent loops, use
After receiving a response, check the finish reason. If it indicates a tool call, extract all invocation blocks from the response.
For each invocation: validate the argument JSON against your local copy of the schema (this catches any schema violations the model may have produced), then route to the matching function.
Append both the assistant's tool-call message and each tool result to the messages list, keyed by call ID. Then call the model again.
Repeat the loop until the finish reason is
(text response) or your step limit is reached. Return the final assistant message.
Example 1 — Data extraction with forced tool use
This pattern uses a tool purely as a schema container. Setting
to the extraction tool's name forces the model to return structured arguments rather than any free text, replacing JSON mode with a typed, named schema.
Example 2 — Multi-tool agent loop with parallel calls (JavaScript)
This example shows how to run an agent loop in JavaScript using the OpenAI SDK, handling multiple parallel tool calls in a single turn.
A customer-support automation platform uses function calling as the backbone of its intent-routing and action-execution layer. When a customer message arrives, the model chooses from tools including
. Each tool's JSON Schema constrains the arguments: refund amounts have a maximum, order IDs must match a pattern, and escalation reasons are an enum.

The orchestrator enforces additional business rules before execution: a refund above $500 always triggers a human-approval step regardless of what the model says; escalation to a specific team requires a matching case category. These rules cannot live inside the model's reasoning — they are encoded in the orchestrator and activated by the structured arguments the model provides.
Mistake 1 — Trusting model arguments without local validation

The model may produce syntactically valid JSON that still violates business rules (negative amounts, unknown IDs, enum values not in your runtime database). Always validate with a schema library before executing side effects.

# Wrong: trust the model directly
args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments)
db.execute("DELETE FROM orders WHERE id = ?", args["order_id"])  # dangerous

# Correct: validate first
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class DeleteArgs(BaseModel):
    order_id: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=50)

safe = DeleteArgs(**json.loads(tc.function.arguments))  # raises if invalid
db.execute("DELETE FROM orders WHERE id = ?", safe.order_id)

Providing 30+ tools in a single call degrades the model's ability to select the right one and increases context token usage. If you need a large capability set, dynamically load only the tools relevant to the current intent (retrieved via a small routing model or keyword search).

# Wrong: send all 50 tools every time
response = client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o", tools=ALL_50_TOOLS, messages=msgs)

# Correct: select relevant tools by intent
intent = classify_intent(user_message)  # e.g., "billing"
relevant_tools = TOOLS_BY_INTENT[intent]  # subset of 3-7 tools
response = client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o", tools=relevant_tools, messages=msgs)
Mistake 3 — Ignoring finish reason and message ordering
Not checking whether the model actually invoked a tool (vs. responding with text) leads to
crashes. Additionally, the OpenAI API requires the assistant's tool-call message to appear in the history
the corresponding tool result messages — omitting it causes an API error on the next request.

# Wrong: assume tool_calls is always present; skip appending assistant turn
args = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0].function.arguments)
messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id, "content": result})  # error!

# Correct: check finish reason, append assistant turn first, then results
msg = response.choices[0].message
messages.append(msg)  # must come first — includes tool_calls
if msg.tool_calls:
    for tc in msg.tool_calls:
        args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments)
        result = dispatch(tc.function.name, args)
        messages.append({"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": tc.id, "content": json.dumps(result)})
else:
    return msg.content  # model chose to reply with text
Each tool should do exactly one thing with a well-defined input/output contract. Avoid
Write descriptions for the model, not for engineers.
field on both the tool and each parameter is read by the model at inference time. Explain
to call the tool, not just
it does. Include format examples in parameter descriptions (e.g., "ISO 8601 date, e.g. 2024-03-15").
wherever the value set is finite and known. This dramatically reduces hallucinated values.
List every mandatory parameter in the
alone to convey requiredness — the schema is the authoritative contract.
Force the tool when extraction is the goal.
to the target tool rather than leaving it on
. The model will never produce a text response when forced, removing an entire class of parsing failures.
Implement a step limit and detect loops.

Agents can enter infinite tool-call loops if they mishandle error results. Always cap the number of iterations (e.g., 15 steps), detect repeated calls with the same arguments, and surface a graceful error rather than running indefinitely.
) rather than raising exceptions in tool handlers. This gives the model a chance to recover by calling a different tool or informing the user, rather than crashing the agent loop.
Log all tool calls and results.

Tool calls are the most observable part of agent behavior. Structured logging (call ID, tool name, arguments, result, latency) enables debugging, cost analysis, and quality evaluation without re-running expensive model calls.

The dominant performance cost in a function-calling workflow is latency: each tool call requires at least one full LLM round-trip plus the actual tool execution time. For N sequential tool calls the total latency is roughly
is the model round-trip (0.5–5 s) and
is tool execution time. Parallel tool calls collapse independent lookups into one model round-trip — always enable concurrent execution at the orchestrator for models that support it (OpenAI's

Token overhead from tool definitions scales with toolset size. Empirically, a single well-written tool definition contributes 50–200 tokens depending on description length and schema complexity. With a 10-tool set, this adds 500–2000 tokens to every request — significant at high call volumes. Strategies to reduce this:
Dynamic tool loading: retrieve only the 3–7 tools relevant to the current intent.
Compact schema descriptions: use terse, factual descriptions instead of multi-sentence explanations; the model generalizes well from concise descriptions.
Tool caching: cache the token counts for each tool definition and use prompt caching (where the provider supports it) to avoid re-tokenizing static tool definitions on every request.

At high call volumes, consider whether some tool calls can be batched. For example, if many independent agent sessions are calling the same read-only tool (e.g., a product lookup), a caching layer in front of the tool function can dramatically reduce downstream API latency without any changes to the agent loop.
fields; parallel tool calls enabled by default; streaming support for
field; native support for streaming tool use blocks.
; function calling available in all Gemini 1.5+ models.
method attaches a tool list to any LLM object and normalizes provider differences. The
and LangGraph provide out-of-the-box agent loops with tool call routing.

# LangChain: bind tools and run a ReAct-style agent
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

@tool
def get_exchange_rate(base: str, quote: str) -> float:
    """Return current exchange rate from base to quote currency."""
    # stub
    return 1.08  # EUR/USD

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
agent = create_react_agent(llm, [get_exchange_rate])

result = agent.invoke({"messages": [("user", "What is EUR/USD rate?")]})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)
Anthropic's Python SDK uses the standard

field. The Pydantic-AI library auto-generates tool schemas directly from Python function signatures and type annotations, eliminating manual JSON Schema authoring and providing runtime argument validation out of the box.
The table below compares function calling with the other main approaches for obtaining structured output from a language model.
JSON Schema on tool parameters; model trained to respect it
Agent action declaration, multi-tool routing, extraction with a named schema
Extra token overhead; round-trips for each call; tool selection can fail
Guarantees valid JSON; no field-level schema
Simple JSON output when schema is enforced in the prompt or post-processed
No field-type or enum guarantees; schema lives in the prompt
Full JSON Schema enforcement on the response itself
OpenAI gpt-4o with structured outputs; limited elsewhere
Typed response object when no action semantics are needed
Newer feature; provider-specific; not an agent-action model
No model-level enforcement; parser enforces post-hoc
Prototyping; simple extraction on low-variance outputs
Fragile; prompt changes break parsing; no schema contract
Provider-agnostic but limited to self-hosted setups; complex setup
Function calling surfaces a new attack surface:

. If a tool fetches external content (web pages, emails, user-uploaded documents) and returns it verbatim, a malicious actor can embed text in that content that instructs the model to call different tools, exfiltrate data, or skip safety checks. The orchestrator must sanitize or summarize external content before returning it as a tool result.
. The model might be manipulated into supplying arguments to a destructive tool (e.g.,

with an admin ID). Defenses include: allowlisting the set of callers and argument values that may trigger each tool, implementing confirmation prompts for destructive actions, and logging every invocation for audit.
Never trust model arguments for operations that modify state. Run Pydantic, JSONSchema, or equivalent validation; reject and report malformed arguments rather than executing partial actions.
Scope tools to the minimum required capability.
A read-only QA agent should not have access to write-path tools. Use separate agent definitions with separate tool sets for different trust levels.
Rate-limit and quota-enforce at the orchestrator.
Agents can call expensive or destructive tools in rapid succession. Enforce per-user, per-session, and per-tool call rate limits outside the model.
Sanitize tool results from untrusted sources.
Before returning web-fetched, email-fetched, or user-provided text as a tool result, strip or summarize it to remove potential injection payloads.
Agent loops can retry on transient failures. If a tool is not idempotent (e.g., "send email"), use an idempotency key derived from the call ID to prevent duplicate execution on retries.
Set hard step limits and monitor for anomalies.
Infinite loops and runaway tool calls are real failure modes. Monitor token spend per agent session and set circuit breakers that halt execution if anomalous call patterns are detected.
Function calling is the standard protocol for combining
in LLM APIs — the model declares intent; the orchestrator executes.
Tool parameter schemas use JSON Schema; the model is trained to respect type, enum, required, and description constraints, making argument output far more reliable than prompt-only approaches.
For pure data extraction, force a tool by name and treat the argument object as the output — no parsing of model text required.
Parallel tool calls eliminate sequential latency for independent lookups; always enable parallel execution at the orchestrator layer.
Schema quality is the single biggest lever on function-calling reliability: specific descriptions, enum constraints, and explicit required arrays reduce hallucinated arguments.

The orchestrator is the security boundary: validate arguments with Pydantic or JSON Schema, sanitize tool results from untrusted sources, and enforce rate limits and step caps before executing any side effect.

Dynamic tool loading — selecting only the relevant 3–7 tools per intent — is the most effective strategy for scaling to large capability sets without degrading model accuracy or inflating context costs.
